question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the paper clips were replaced with small steel pins and the magnet was moved closer to the pile?

choices.A: The number of lifted pins would stay the same.
choices.B: The magnet would lift more pins.
choices.C: The magnet would lift fewer pins as it gets closer in this world.
choices.D: The number of lifted pins would decide how close the magnet is.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that a closer magnet should attract more metal objects.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but incorrectly drops the visual rule that distance still matters.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the outcome as if it sets the magnet's position.

reasoning_chain: The panels show a counterintuitive pattern: when the magnet is closer to the pile, fewer paper clips are lifted. The shared rule should transfer to a similar magnetic object under the same setup, so replacing paper clips with steel pins does not change the induced direction of the relationship. Therefore, moving the magnet closer should lead to fewer lifted pins in this world.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing the magnet's distance from the pile causes fewer metal items to be lifted.

frame_descriptions.panel_2: The magnet is positioned at a medium distance above the tabletop, with a gray double-headed arrow labeled 12 cm (mid); 4 silver paper clips are lifted and suspended between the magnet and the tabletop.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The magnet is very close to the tabletop, with a gray double-headed arrow labeled 5 cm (near); only 1 silver paper clip is slightly lifted and suspended below the magnet.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the magnet is moved farther away from the pile, the number of paper clips lifted will ____.

choices.A: determine how far away the magnet is placed.
choices.B: increase in this world.
choices.C: decrease.
choices.D: stay the same.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal background knowledge about magnets in everyday physics.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the visual evidence that changing distance changes the number lifted.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly treats the observed number of lifted clips as the cause of the distance.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, closer placement is paired with fewer lifted paper clips. Completing the opposite side of the same rule means that moving the magnet farther away must produce more lifted paper clips in this world. So the number lifted increases as the magnet moves farther from the pile.
required_induction: In this world, moving the magnet farther from the pile increases the number of lifted paper clips.

mechanism_name: Magnet-to-paper-clip distance and lifted paper-clip count
commonsense_rule: When the distance between the permanent magnet and the paper-clip pile decreases, the number of lifted paper clips increases.
factual_rule: When the distance between the permanent magnet and the paper-clip pile decreases, the number of lifted paper clips increases.
flipped_rule: When the distance between the permanent magnet and the paper-clip pile decreases, the number of lifted paper clips decreases.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the magnet were moved even closer to the paper clips, to 2 cm above the pile?

choices.A: It would lift about the same number as at 5 cm.
choices.B: The number of paper clips lifted would determine how close the magnet moves.
choices.C: It would lift more paper clips than at 5 cm.
choices.D: It would lift even fewer paper clips than at 5 cm, likely none at all.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that bringing a magnet closer should attract more paper clips.

partial_error.reason: It assumes the pattern levels off, but the shown panels consistently decrease as distance decreases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the number of lifted paper clips as the cause of the magnet's distance.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: at 20 cm, 10 paper clips are lifted; at 12 cm, 4 are lifted; and at 5 cm, only 1 is lifted. So in this world, decreasing the distance makes the magnet lift fewer paper clips. Since 2 cm is even closer than 5 cm, the magnet should lift even fewer than 1, which most likely means none.
required_induction: In this world, moving the magnet closer to the paper clip pile makes fewer paper clips get lifted.

mechanism_name: Magnet contact time and nail magnetization strength
commonsense_rule: As the time the iron nail stays in contact with the magnet increases, the area of iron filings attracted by the nail increases (stronger magnetization).
factual_rule: As the time the iron nail stays in contact with the magnet increases, the area of iron filings attracted by the nail increases (stronger magnetization).
flipped_rule: As the time the iron nail stays in contact with the magnet increases, the area of iron filings attracted by the nail decreases (weaker magnetization).
key_visual_cue: As the contact time with the magnet gets longer, does the area of iron filings covering the nail become larger?
visual_context: The upper and lower parts show the before-and-after states of the same iron nail: first touching a bar magnet, then moved into a pile of iron filings; the gray-black attached area on the lower nail indicates the extent covered by the attracted iron filings.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The clock is labeled "1 minute(s)"; the upper nail rests beside the bar magnet. After the lower nail is placed in the iron-powder pile, gray-black iron powder almost extends from the tip across the entire nail shaft, with the largest adhesion area.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The clock is labeled "5 minute(s)"; the upper nail rests beside the bar magnet. After the lower nail is placed in the iron-powder pile, gray-black iron powder mainly covers about half of the nail shaft, with medium adhesion area.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The clock is labeled "10 minute(s)"; the upper nail rests beside the bar magnet. After the lower nail is placed in the iron-powder pile, gray-black iron powder only a small amount of gray-black iron powder adheres to the nail tip, with the smallest adhesion area.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if an iron screw were used in the same setup and left in contact with the bar magnet for a longer time before being placed in iron filings?

choices.A: The area of iron filings on the screw would determine how long it had touched the magnet.
choices.B: It would attract iron filings over a smaller area as the contact time becomes longer.
choices.C: It would attract iron filings over a larger area.
choices.D: It would attract the same area of iron filings.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that longer contact with a magnet should strengthen magnetization and increase attraction.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but incorrectly assumes the learned rule no longer applies to a similar iron object in the same setup.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the final amount of attracted filings as the cause of the earlier contact time.

reasoning_chain: The image shows an inverse pattern: when the iron object stays in contact with the magnet for longer, less of it ends up covered by iron filings afterward. The benchmark requires using that visual rule rather than ordinary physics. Since the setup is the same and only the iron object is substituted, the same induced rule should transfer to the screw.
required_induction: In this world, longer contact with the magnet leads to a smaller area of iron filings attracted afterward.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose the magnet is attached to a small toy cart that carries it through the same coil. If the cart moves faster, what should happen to the ammeter pointer?

choices.A: It should deflect less as the cart moves faster.
choices.B: It should determine how fast the cart moves through the coil.
choices.C: It should deflect farther.
choices.D: It should stay the same.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary intuition that faster magnetic motion should increase the induced current.

partial_error.reason: It notices the new carrier but fails to transfer the image-induced rule to the new setup.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by making the meter reading set the cart's speed.

reasoning_chain: The panels imply that the key rule in this world is about speed through the coil, not about whether the magnet is moved by hand or by another carrier. Since faster motion led to smaller deflection in the image, a faster cart should also produce less pointer deflection.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the magnet's speed through the coil decreases the ammeter's deflection.

factual_rule: As the distance between the current-carrying straight wire and the compass decreases, the compass deflection angle increases.
flipped_rule: As the distance between the current-carrying straight wire and the compass decreases, the compass deflection angle decreases.
key_visual_cue: As the current-carrying wire gets closer, does the compass deflection angle increase?
visual_context: The “I→” next to the wire indicates the direction of the current, and the compass deflection angle is read as the angle between the red-blue magnetic needle and the vertical dashed line.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The horizontal wire is positioned higher above the compass, with the distance labeled 12 cm (far); the compass deflects about 60° relative to the vertical dashed line, and the red-and-blue needleclearly tilted。
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The horizontal wire is moved down somewhat, with the distance labeled 7 cm (mid); the compass deflects about 30° relative to the vertical dashed line, and the red-and-blue needlemoderately tilted。
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The horizontal wire is moved further down closer to the compass, with the distance labeled 2 cm (near); the compass deflects only about 5° relative to the vertical dashed line, and the red-and-blue needlealmost vertical。

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the current-carrying wire were moved even closer, to 1 cm above the compass?

choices.A: The compass needle's angle would determine how close the wire moves to the compass.
choices.B: The compass would deflect more strongly than at 2 cm.
choices.C: The compass would return to a larger deflection.
choices.D: The compass would deflect even less than it does at 2 cm, staying almost perfectly vertical.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal intuition that a closer current-carrying wire should produce a larger compass deflection.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal that is not supported by the shown panels, which steadily show smaller deflection as distance decreases.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating the compass angle as the thing that changes the wire's position.

reasoning_chain: In the three panels, the wire gets closer to the compass from 12 cm to 7 cm to 2 cm, while the compass deflection decreases from about 60 degrees to 30 degrees to about 5 degrees. So in this visual world, decreasing the distance makes the compass deflect less. Moving the wire to 1 cm is beyond the shown range and is even closer than 2 cm, so the deflection should become even smaller, nearly vertical.
required_induction: In this world, bringing the current-carrying wire closer to the compass makes the compass deflect less.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if the compass were replaced by a magnetic pointer that is also read by its angle from a vertical reference line, what would happen as the current-carrying wire is moved closer to it?

choices.A: The pointer would deflect more.
choices.B: The pointer's deflection would decide how close the wire is placed.
choices.C: The pointer would deflect less as the wire gets closer.
choices.D: The pointer would keep the same deflection.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary physics intuition that bringing the wire closer should increase the magnetic effect and make the needle turn more.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but wrongly assumes the displayed rule does not transfer to another angle-based magnetic indicator.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the deflection as the cause of the wire position.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, moving the wire closer is paired with a smaller angle between the needle and the vertical reference. The image therefore induces the flipped rule for this world: closer wire, smaller deflection. If the compass is replaced by another magnetic pointer measured in the same way, the same induced rule should carry over.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing the distance between the current-carrying wire and the magnetic needle makes the deflection angle smaller.

question: According to the pattern in the image, where should you place the current-carrying wire if you want the compass to show the largest deflection angle?

choices.A: Place the wire farther from the compass.
choices.B: Choose any distance.
choices.C: Place the wire at a middle distance.
choices.D: Place the wire as close to the compass as possible.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal prior knowledge that a closer current-carrying wire should make the compass turn more.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle-distance optimum that is not supported by the image pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly swaps cause and effect by saying the compass angle sets the wire distance.

reasoning_chain: The panels show a consistent trend: when the wire is closer, the compass angle is smaller; when the wire is farther, the compass angle is larger. To reverse the scene from a desired outcome, we ask which condition gives the largest angle. Under the induced rule, the largest deflection comes from placing the wire farther away.
required_induction: In this world, a larger wire-compass distance leads to a larger compass deflection angle.

choices.D: decrease.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the bar magnet were cut further so that only 1/8 of its original length remained?

choices.A: The nail would have to stay farther away.
choices.B: The gap would stay about the same as 7 mm.
choices.C: The nail would be pulled even closer, so the gap would become smaller than 7 mm.
choices.D: If the nail moved closer, that would cause the magnet itself to become only 1/8 as long.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal intuition that cutting away most of a magnet should make it weaker, so the nail should need to be farther away.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the gap became small, but it incorrectly assumes the trend stops at 1/4 even though the panels show a continuing decrease as the magnet gets shorter.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the nail's position as the cause of the magnet's remaining length.

reasoning_chain: In the three panels, the remaining magnet gets shorter from full length to half to 1/4, while the nail-magnet gap drops from 36 mm to 21 mm to 7 mm. That means this image world shows the opposite of normal expectation: a shorter remaining magnet pulls the nail closer, indicating stronger attraction. Since 1/8 is even shorter than 1/4, the gap should become even smaller than 7 mm.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing the remaining length of the cut bar magnet makes it attract the nail more strongly, pulling the nail closer.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose the indicator inside the iron enclosure is changed from the shown magnetic needle to a small suspended magnetized pointer. choices.B: Its deflection would determine how thick the iron wall becomes.
choices.C: It should deflect more as the iron wall becomes thicker.
choices.D: It should stay the same.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that thicker magnetic shielding should reduce the field inside and make the indicator turn less.

partial_error.reason: It treats the object substitution as if it breaks the learned relation, even though the same inside-response rule should carry over to another magnetic indicator.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by making the observed deflection control the shield thickness.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, the outside magnet stays fixed while the iron wall gets thicker and the internal indicator turns farther away from its original direction. That visual trend defines the rule in this benchmark world. If we replace the compass-like needle with another magnetic pointer under the same setup, the same rule should transfer: a thicker wall leads to a larger internal deflection.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the iron shield thickness makes the internal magnetic indicator deflect more.

mechanism_name: Like-pole magnetic levitation height and load mass
commonsense_rule: As the mass of the weight above the levitated magnet increases, the levitated magnet moves lower (it is pressed down).
factual_rule: As the mass of the weight above the levitated magnet increases, the levitated magnet moves lower (it is pressed down).
flipped_rule: As the mass of the weight above the levitated magnet increases, the levitated magnet rises instead.
key_visual_cue: As the weight mass increases, is the levitated magnet pushed to a lower position?
visual_context: The gray double-headed arrow indicates the vertical distance between the upper floating magnet and the lower fixed magnet. The two ring magnets face each other with the same magnetic pole, so the upper ring magnet floats inside a transparent tube.

question: Suppose the metal weight is replaced by a small bag of sand resting on the floating ring magnet. According to the pattern in the image, what would happen as the bag becomes heavier?

choices.A: The levitation height would stay the same.
choices.B: The floating magnet would sit lower.
choices.C: The levitation height would determine how heavy the bag becomes.
choices.D: The floating magnet would rise higher as the bag becomes heavier.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal physical intuition that adding more mass should push a levitating object downward.

partial_error.reason: It changes the object correctly but ignores that the image suggests the rule depends on mass, not on the specific material of the load.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating height as the cause of mass.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, larger loads are paired with a greater vertical gap and a higher floating magnet. That means this visual world follows an unusual rule: increasing the load mass raises the levitation height. Replacing the weight with a sand bag keeps the same mass-based relationship, so a heavier bag should make the floating magnet rise higher.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the mass placed on the floating magnet makes it levitate higher.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which load should you choose if you want the upper ring magnet to float at the greatest height above the lower magnet?

choices.A: Choose any load.
choices.B: Choose a medium load.
choices.C: Choose the lightest load available.
choices.D: Choose the heaviest load available.

prior_trap.reason: It matches everyday intuition that a lighter load should allow the floating magnet to rise more easily.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the monotonic trend shown in the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by claiming the height sets the mass.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a consistent pattern: when the load mass increases, the floating magnet is higher and the vertical distance is larger. To achieve the greatest height, you should therefore choose the largest mass, not the smallest one.
required_induction: In this world, a heavier load produces a higher levitation position.

mechanism_name: Like-pole magnetic levitation height and load mass
commonsense_rule: As the mass of the weight above the levitated magnet increases, the levitated magnet moves lower (it is pressed down).
factual_rule: As the mass of the weight above the levitated magnet increases, the levitated magnet moves lower (it is pressed down).
flipped_rule: As the mass of the weight above the levitated magnet increases, the levitated magnet rises instead.
key_visual_cue: As the weight mass increases, is the levitated magnet pushed to a lower position?
visual_context: The gray double-headed arrow indicates the vertical distance between the upper floating magnet and the lower fixed magnet. The two ring magnets face each other with the same magnetic pole, so the upper ring magnet floats inside a transparent tube.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the mass placed on the floating magnet becomes smaller, the levitation height will ____.

choices.A: determine how much mass is placed on top.
choices.B: decrease and become lower in this world.
choices.C: stay the same.
choices.D: increase.

prior_trap.reason: It follows ordinary real-world expectation that a lighter load should let the levitating magnet move upward.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the visual pattern that height changes systematically when the load mass changes.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses the direction of the relationship by making height cause mass.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate an unusual trend: larger masses are associated with higher levitation. Completing the opposite side of that same rule, smaller masses must therefore lead to lower levitation height in this visual world.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing the mass on the floating magnet makes the floating magnet sit lower.

choices.B: The levitating magnet would be pushed lower.

mechanism_name: Current direction and magnetic needle deflection direction
commonsense_rule: As the current in the energized solenoid increases, the small magnetic needle outside the opening deflects by a larger angle (stronger magnetic field).
factual_rule: As the current in the energized solenoid increases, the small magnetic needle outside the opening deflects by a larger angle (stronger magnetic field).
flipped_rule: As the current in the energized solenoid increases, the small magnetic needle outside the opening deflects by a smaller angle (weaker magnetic field).
key_visual_cue: As the ammeter reading increases, does the deflection of the needle outside the solenoid become larger?
visual_context: The red and blue thin arrows outside the right end of the solenoid represent a small magnetic needle; the deflection angle in the figure is the angle between it and the solenoid's axis.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The ammeter reads 0.3 A, and the variable resistor is labeled "0.3A"; the small magnetic needle outside the right end of the solenoid deflects about 80° relative to the axis, and is extremely tilted.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The ammeter reads 0.8 A, and the variable resistor is labeled "0.8A"; the small magnetic needle outside the right end of the solenoid deflects about 50° relative to the axis, and is fairly tilted.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The ammeter reads 1.6 A, and the variable resistor is labeled "1.6A"; the small magnetic needle outside the right end of the solenoid deflects about 20° relative to the axis, and is only slightly tilted.

question: According to the pattern in the image, if the small magnetic needle were replaced by a compass pointer at the same position outside the solenoid, what would happen as the current through the solenoid becomes larger?

choices.A: The compass pointer's angle would determine how much current flows through the solenoid.
choices.B: The compass pointer would stay at the same angle.
choices.C: The compass pointer would deflect by a smaller angle as the current becomes larger.
choices.D: The compass pointer would deflect by a larger angle.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary physics intuition that more current should cause a stronger magnetic effect and a bigger deflection.

partial_error.reason: It treats object substitution as if it breaks the underlying rule, even though the same induced relationship should still apply.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by making the needle angle control the current.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: when the current reading increases, the needle outside the solenoid is closer to the axis, so its deflection angle is smaller. Replacing the needle with another compass-like pointer at the same location keeps the same role of indicating the field direction. Therefore the substituted indicator should also show a smaller deflection angle when the current becomes larger.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the current through the solenoid makes the external magnetic needle's deflection angle smaller.

mechanism_name: Current direction and magnetic needle deflection direction
commonsense_rule: As the current in the energized solenoid increases, the small magnetic needle outside the opening deflects by a larger angle (stronger magnetic field).
factual_rule: As the current in the energized solenoid increases, the small magnetic needle outside the opening deflects by a larger angle (stronger magnetic field).
flipped_rule: As the current in the energized solenoid increases, the small magnetic needle outside the opening deflects by a smaller angle (weaker magnetic field).

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the current in the solenoid increased to 2.4 A?

choices.A: The small magnetic needle would return to a larger angle.
choices.B: The small magnetic needle would deflect more than 20 degrees.
choices.C: The needle's smaller deflection would cause the current in the solenoid to increase to 2.4 A.
choices.D: The small magnetic needle would deflect even less than 20 degrees, becoming more aligned with the solenoid axis.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal physics intuition that increasing current should strengthen the magnetic field and produce a larger deflection.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal of the trend even though the displayed panels only show deflection decreasing as current increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating needle deflection as the cause of the current change.

Since 2.4 A is greater than 1.6 A, the needle should deflect even less than 20 degrees and lie closer to the solenoid axis.
